title: Commission to Study and Develop Reparation Proposals for African Americans Act
short_title: Commission to Study and Develop Reparation Proposals for African Americans Act
plain_summary: This bill creates a 13-person commission to study slavery in America from 1619 to 1865 and how it still affects African Americans today. The commission will look at laws and policies that hurt formerly enslaved people and their families after slavery ended. They will recommend ways for the United States to make up for the harm caused by slavery and discrimination, which could include an official apology and payments called reparations. The commission has one year to finish their work and report back to Congress, with 12 million dollars provided to fund their research.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.
policy_area: Civil Rights and Liberties, Minority Issues
subjects: War crimes, genocide, crimes against humanity

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Commission to Study and Develop Reparation Proposals for African Americans Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Findings and Purpose
sections.text: Congress has found that about four million Africans and their children were enslaved in the United States and the colonies that became the United States from sixteen nineteen to eighteen sixty five. The United States government officially allowed slavery in its laws and constitution from seventeen eighty nine through eighteen sixty five. Slavery in the United States was wrong and cruel. It took away African people's lives, freedom, rights as citizens, and their cultural traditions. It also denied them the rewards of their own work. There is plenty of evidence from scholars, legal documents, community records, and popular culture that shows slavery and discrimination still affect African Americans today. After slavery ended, federal, state, and local governments continued to support practices that hurt and disadvantaged African Americans. These included sharecropping (a system where people worked land but could not own it), convict leasing (forcing prisoners to work for private companies), Jim Crow laws (laws that separated Black and white people), redlining (refusing to give loans in Black neighborhoods), unequal education, and unfair treatment by police and courts. Because of this historic and ongoing discrimination, African Americans continue to face serious economic, educational, and health problems. More than one million Black people are in prison or under court supervision. The unemployment rate for Black people is more than twice as high as for white people. On average, Black families have less than one sixteenth of the wealth that white families have, and this gap has gotten worse over time. The purpose of this law is to create a commission that will study slavery from fifteen sixty five in colonial Florida and from sixteen nineteen in other colonies through eighteen sixty five. This includes how federal and state governments supported slavery in their laws and constitutions. The commission will also study legal and unofficial discrimination against freed slaves and their children from the end of the Civil War to today. This includes discrimination in economics, politics, education, and social life. The commission will look at how slavery and discrimination still hurt living African Americans and society today. It will examine how teaching materials and digital resources are being used to deny how cruel slavery was and that it was a crime against humanity. It will study how Northern states helped with Southern slavery and how institutions like colleges, corporations, and religious groups directly benefited from slavery. The commission will recommend ways to educate Americans about what it finds and suggest appropriate remedies. Finally, it will give Congress a report with its findings and recommendations.

sections.title: Section 3 Establishment and Duties
sections.text: This law creates the Commission to Study and Develop Reparation Proposals for African Americans. The commission must do several important jobs. First, it must identify, collect, and organize all the evidence about slavery that existed from fifteen sixty five in colonial Florida and from sixteen nineteen in other colonies through eighteen sixty five. This includes facts about how Africans were captured and obtained, how they were transported to the United States and colonies to be enslaved and how they were treated during transport, how Africans were sold and bought as property between states and within states, how African slaves were treated in the colonies and United States including taking away their freedom and exploiting their labor and destroying their culture language religion and families, the widespread denial of their humanity and sexual abuse and treating them as property, how federal and state governments supported slavery through constitutional and legal provisions including how much these governments prevented opposed or restricted efforts by formerly enslaved Africans and their descendants to return to their homeland, and federal and state laws that discriminated against formerly enslaved Africans and their descendants who became United States citizens from eighteen sixty eight to today. Second, the commission must identify other forms of discrimination in public and private sectors against freed African slaves and their descendants who became United States citizens from eighteen sixty eight to today. This includes redlining, unequal education funding, and predatory financial practices. Third, it must identify the ongoing negative effects of slavery and discrimination on living African Americans and on society in the United States. Fourth, it must recommend appropriate ways to educate the American public about what the commission finds. Fifth, it must recommend appropriate remedies based on what the commission finds. When making these recommendations, the commission must address several questions. It must consider how its recommendations compare with international standards for fixing wrongs and injuries caused by foreign governments including full reparations and special measures as understood by various international agreements laws and findings. It must consider how the federal government will offer a formal apology on behalf of the American people for committing gross human rights violations and crimes against humanity against African slaves and their descendants. It must consider how federal laws and policies that continue to unfairly and negatively affect African Americans as a group and those that continue the lingering effects both materially and psychologically can be eliminated. It must consider how the injuries from slavery and discrimination can be reversed and provide appropriate policies programs projects and recommendations for reversing these injuries. It must consider how any form of payment to the descendants of enslaved Africans should be calculated based on the commission's findings. It must determine what form of payment should be awarded, through what organizations such payment should be given, and who should be eligible for such payment. It must consider whether any other measures of rehabilitation or making things right to African descendants is warranted and what form and scope those measures should take. The commission must give Congress a written report of its findings and recommendations no later than one year after its first meeting.

sections.title: Section 4 Membership
sections.text: The commission will have thirteen members who must be appointed within ninety days after this law is passed. The President will appoint three members. The Speaker of the House of Representatives will appoint three members. The President pro tempore of the Senate (the senator who leads the Senate when the Vice President is not there) will appoint one member. Six members will be selected from major civil society and reparations organizations that have historically championed the cause of reparatory justice (groups that work for fair treatment and making things right for past wrongs). All commission members must be people who are especially qualified to serve because of their education, training, activism, or experience, particularly in African American studies and reparatory justice. Commission members will serve for the entire life of the commission. If a member leaves, the vacancy will be filled in the same way the original appointment was made. The President must call the first commission meeting within one hundred twenty days after this law is passed or within thirty days after Congress approves money to fund this law, whichever comes later. Seven members make a quorum (the minimum number needed to conduct business), but fewer members can hold hearings. The commission will elect a Chair and Vice Chair from its members who will serve for the life of the commission. Commission members will be paid at a daily rate not exceeding the daily equivalent of the annual rate for a level four Executive Schedule position under federal law for each day they work for the commission including travel time. However, commission members who are already full time federal employees or Members of Congress will receive no additional pay allowances or benefits for their commission service. All commission members will be reimbursed for travel living expenses and other necessary expenses as authorized by federal law.

sections.title: Section 5 Powers of the Commission
sections.text: The commission can hold hearings and meet at any times and places in the United States to carry out this law. It can request witnesses to attend and testify and ask for books records correspondence memos papers and documents that the commission thinks are appropriate. The commission can ask a federal district court to require by subpoena (a legal order) or otherwise such attendance testimony or production of documents. Any subcommittee or member of the commission can take any action the full commission is authorized to take if the commission gives permission. The commission can get information directly from the head of any department agency or office of the federal executive branch that the commission thinks is useful for its work. All federal departments agencies and offices must cooperate with the commission regarding such information and must provide all information requested by the commission to the extent permitted by law.

sections.title: Section 6 Administrative Provisions
sections.text: The commission can hire and set pay for staff that it thinks is appropriate. Commission staff can be hired without following the usual federal civil service rules about competitive hiring and without following federal rules about job classification and pay scales. However, no commission staff member can be paid more than the daily equivalent of the annual rate for a level four Executive Schedule position under federal law. The commission can hire experts and consultants following federal law but at rates not exceeding the daily equivalent of the highest rate payable under federal pay scales. The commission can make agreements with the Administrator of General Services (the federal office that provides support services) to get financial and administrative services needed to do the commission's work. Payment for such services will be made by reimbursement from commission funds in amounts agreed upon by the commission Chair and the Administrator. The commission can buy supplies services and property by contract following applicable laws and regulations and to the extent that Congress provides money. The commission can make contracts with federal departments agencies and offices, state agencies, and private companies institutions and agencies to conduct research or surveys prepare reports and other activities necessary for the commission's work to the extent that Congress provides money.

sections.title: Section 7 Termination
sections.text: The commission will end ninety days after it submits its report to Congress.

sections.title: Section 8 Authorization of Appropriations
sections.text: To carry out this law, Congress is authorized to spend twelve million dollars.
